networks@rag_mixed::+reranker.question: How does retransmit-on-duplicate differ from retransmit-on-timeout?
networks@rag_mixed::+reranker.answer:
**Retransmit‑on‑timeout** 
- The sender (or receiver) resends a packet only when no acknowledgment is received before a timer expires — i.e., the packet is presumed lost after the timeout interval [Source 3]. 
- At least one side of a connection must use this policy; otherwise both ends could wait forever for a response [Source 1].

**Retransmit‑on‑duplicate** 
- The side that implements this policy reacts to receiving a *duplicate* packet (e.g., a second copy of Data[N]) by retransmitting the appropriate acknowledgment (or the data itself) [Source 2]. 
- It is typically used by the receiver, and the other side can rely on it instead of (or in addition to) timeout‑based retransmission [Source 1]. 

**Key difference** 
- Timeout‑based retransmission is triggered by the *absence* of an ACK within a time window, whereas duplicate‑based retransmission is triggered by the *presence* of a duplicate packet that indicates a previous transmission was already received. [Source 1][Source 2]

networks@rag_mixed::+reranker.question: Why is stop-and-wait inefficient, and how do sliding windows improve on it?
networks@rag_mixed::+reranker.answer:
**Stop‑and‑wait is inefficient because the sender is allowed to have only one packet “in flight.”** 
The text explains that in stop‑and‑wait “the sender sends only one outstanding packet at a time… each side has only one packet in play at a time” and that “Stop‑and‑wait is reliable but it is not very efficient (unless the path involves neither intermediate switches nor significant propagation delay; that is, the path involves a single LAN link). Most links along a multi‑hop stop‑and‑wait path will be idle most of the time”【Source 1】. 

**Sliding windows improve throughput by permitting several packets to be sent before an acknowledgment is required.** 
The sliding‑window algorithm “allows the sender to continue to transmit, sending Data[N+1] (and beyond) **without** waiting for ACK[N]” and the sender may send a range of packets “last_ACKed + 1 through last_ACKed + winsize”【Source 2】. As acknowledgments arrive, “the window slides forward… freeing the sender to send more packets,” keeping the network links busy and reducing idle time.

networks@rag_mixed::+reranker.question: What does the acronym ARQ mean and how does the mechanism work?
networks@rag_mixed::+reranker.answer:
**ARQ** stands for **Automatic Repeat reQuest**. 
It implements a **retransmit‑on‑timeout** policy: when a packet is sent, the sender waits for an acknowledgment (ACK); if no ACK is received before a timeout expires, the packet is resent [Source 1]. 
The protocol typically uses sequence numbers so that the sender can match ACKs to the transmitted packets. In the simplest “stop‑and‑wait” form of ARQ, the sender transmits only one packet at a time and does not send the next packet until the ACK for the current packet is received [Source 1].

networks@rag_mixed::+reranker.question: How do you make data transport reliable?
networks@rag_mixed::+reranker.answer:
Reliable data transport is built by using an **ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest) protocol** that employs a *retransmit‑on‑timeout* policy: each packet is given a sequence number, the sender waits for an acknowledgment (ACK) and, if no ACK arrives before a timeout expires, the packet is resent [Source 1]. 
In the simplest form—**stop‑and‑wait**—the sender transmits only one packet at a time and does not send the next packet until it receives the ACK for the current one; this guarantees that every packet is either acknowledged or retransmitted [Source 1]. 
More efficient reliability is achieved with **sliding windows**, which allow the sender to have several packets “in flight” while still requiring cumulative ACKs; the sender tracks the last acknowledged packet ( `last_ACKed` ) and slides the window forward as new ACKs arrive, retransmitting any packet whose ACK is lost or whose timeout expires [Source 1][Source 3]. 
The diagrams of lost data, lost ACK, and delayed ACK illustrate how the retransmit‑on‑timeout mechanism recovers from packet loss or timing anomalies [Source 2].

networks@rag_mixed::+reranker.question: How do the bottleneck link, RTT_noLoad, and the ideal window size relate to each other?
networks@rag_mixed::+reranker.answer:
The bottleneck link determines the **available bandwidth**. 
The “no‑load” round‑trip time, **RTTₙₒₗₒad**, is the pure propagation delay of the path when no packets are queued. 

In a steady‑state connection the window size that exactly fills the bottleneck without creating a queue is the product of the bottleneck bandwidth and the no‑load RTT:

\[
\text{ideal winsize}= \text{bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}_{\text{noLoad}}
\]

* If the sender’s window is **smaller** than this product, throughput is limited by the window size (winsize < bandwidth × RTTₙₒₗₒa d) [Source 2]. 
* If the window is **equal to or larger** than the product, the connection can use the full capacity of the bottleneck link; any excess causes packets to queue and RTT inflates until winsize = bandwidth × RTTₐ𝚌𝚝𝚞𝚊𝚕 [Source 2]. 

Thus, the bottleneck bandwidth and RTTₙₒₗₒa d together set the **ideal window size** that maximizes throughput without incurring queuing delay. [Source 1]

networks@rag_mixed::+compression.question: Why is stop-and-wait inefficient, and how do sliding windows improve on it?
networks@rag_mixed::+compression.answer:
**Stop‑and‑wait is inefficient** because the sender is allowed to have only one packet “in flight.” As a result, most links on a multi‑hop path sit idle while the sender waits for an acknowledgment, especially when there are intermediate switches or significant propagation delay【Source 1】. 

**Sliding windows improve throughput** by letting the sender transmit several packets before needing an ACK. The sender chooses a window size and can send all packets from `last_ACKed + 1` up to `last_ACKed + winsize`; each received ACK slides the window forward and frees the sender to issue more packets, keeping the network links busy【Source 2】.

networks@rag_mixed::+compression.question: How do you make data transport reliable?
networks@rag_mixed::+compression.answer:
**Reliable data transport is achieved by combining retransmission mechanisms with a sliding‑window protocol**:

- The transport layer resends a packet when no acknowledgment is received before a timeout expires – a *retransmit‑on‑timeout* policy [Source 1]. 
- This requires each packet to carry a sequence number and the receiver to send an ACK for that number [Source 1]. 
- Protocols that use this policy are called **ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest)** protocols [Source 1]. 
- Either endpoint may implement retransmit‑on‑timeout, and the receiver can also use a *retransmit‑on‑duplicate* strategy when it receives a duplicate packet [Source 2]. 
- To keep the network busy, a **sliding‑window** algorithm lets the sender transmit several packets without waiting for each individual ACK [Source 1]; the sender is allowed to have up to *winsize* unacknowledged packets in flight [Source 3]. 
- ACKs are **cumulative** – an ACK N confirms receipt of all packets ≤ N, so a lost ACK can be compensated by a later ACK (N + 1) [Source 3]. 
- When an ACK arrives with a number greater than the last acknowledged packet, the window slides forward, updating the “last_ACKed” variable and permitting new packets to be sent [Source 3].

Together, these mechanisms (retransmit‑on‑timeout/duplicate and sliding windows with cumulative ACKs) provide reliable data transport over an unreliable lower layer.

networks@rag_mixed::+compression.question: How do the bottleneck link, RTT_noLoad, and the ideal window size relate to each other?
networks@rag_mixed::+compression.answer:
The bottleneck link determines the **bandwidth** that can be carried. 
The “no‑load” round‑trip time, **RTTₙₒₗₒa𝑑**, is the time a packet would need to travel the path when no packets are queued. 
The product of these two quantities gives the **bandwidth‑delay product**, which is the number of packets that can be in transit without any queuing:

- “the number of packets actually in transit (not queued) … will always be bandwidth × RTTₙₒₗₒa𝑑”【Source 1】. 

If the sender’s window size is **smaller** than this product, the connection is limited by the window size and does not fully use the bottleneck link:

- “winsize < bandwidth × RTTₙₒₗₒa𝑑 – then winsize is the limiting factor in throughput”【Source 2】. 

When the window size is **equal to or larger** than the bandwidth‑delay product, the bottleneck link is fully utilized; any excess window size merely creates queueing and inflates the actual RTT:

- “winsize ≥ bandwidth × RTTₙₒₗₒa𝑑 … the connection is using 100 % of the capacity of the bottleneck link”【Source 2】. 
- The figure on page 8 shows that a window size of 6 packets (the product of bandwidth = 6 packets/RTT and RTTₙₒₗₒa𝑑 = 1 RTT) yields 100 % utilization【Source 3】. 

Thus, the **ideal window size** is exactly the bottleneck bandwidth multiplied by RTTₙₒₗₒa𝑑 (the bandwidth‑delay product). Smaller windows under‑utilize the link; larger windows cause queuing but do not increase throughput beyond the bottleneck capacity.
